summary:
Ain Ghazal, located on the outskirts of modern Amman in Jordan, was one of the largest known Neolithic settlements in the ancient Near East, with a population estimated at several thousand people at its peak. Excavations at the site have yielded a remarkable set of large plaster statues dating to approximately 7000–6500 BCE, making them among the oldest large-scale sculptural works ever discovered. The statues were created by building reed armatures coated with lime plaster and painting with natural pigments, and they depict human figures with prominently rendered eyes.

The Ain Ghazal statues were found buried in two separate caches beneath the floors of the settlement, suggesting deliberate and ceremonial deposition. Their size — some stand nearly a meter tall — and the sophistication of their construction indicate that they played an important role in the ritual or religious life of the community. The figures may represent ancestors, deities, or supernatural beings central to the spiritual world of early Neolithic peoples in the Levant.

The statues are significant not only as works of art but as evidence of the complex symbolic and religious life of pre-urban communities. Their discovery has contributed to a growing recognition that large-scale organized religion and community ritual preceded the development of cities and formal states, challenging earlier assumptions that cultural complexity was a product of urbanism rather than its precondition.
